perturbation.original_text: The Company shall comply with all securities regulatory filing requirements on a timely basis in connection with the issuance of any Equity Securities of the Company to any HOC Entity, including filing within the periods stipulated under Securities Laws, at the Company's expense, all private placement forms required to be filed by the Company and paying all filing fees required to be paid in connection therewith so that such issuance may lawfully occur without the necessity of filing a prospectus, registration statement or any similar document under the Securities Laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company shall endeavor to comply with securities regulatory filing requirements in connection with the issuance of any Equity Securities of the Company to any HOC Entity, including filing private placement forms and paying filing fees, but the specific timing and extent of such compliance shall be determined at the Company's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall comply' to 'shall endeavor to comply' and adding that 'the specific timing and extent of such compliance shall be determined at the Company's discretion' introduces ambiguity, undermining the mandatory nature of securities filings. This makes compliance discretionary, potentially leading to delays or incomplete filings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 10b-5 prohibits making false or misleading statements or omissions of material facts in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. By making compliance discretionary, the company could delay or avoid disclosing material information through required filings, potentially misleading investors and violating Rule 10b-5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall comply' to 'shall endeavor to comply,' coupled with the discretion granted to the company regarding the 'timing and extent of such compliance,' directly contradicts the obligation to comply with securities regulatory filing requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits omissions of material facts. By making compliance discretionary, the company opens itself to potentially delaying or avoiding disclosing material information, which could violate this rule. The original text mandated compliance, whereas the changed text weakens that obligation to a mere 'endeavor,' effectively creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Company shall, to the extent and for so long as HOC Entities hold at least 14.5% of the Shares on a non-diluted basis, upon HOC's request, permit representatives of the HOC Entities to have access to the site and any of the premises where the business and operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries are conducted and access and duplicating rights (and use commercially reasonable efforts to cause persons or firms possessing such documentation or information to give similar access and duplicating rights) to the Company and its Subsidiaries' books of account and records and such other documents, communications, items and matters, within the knowledge, possession or control of the Company, which HOC may reasonably request, at HOC's own cost (other than those it is permitted to examine and make copies of free of charge pursuant to applicable Laws) provided that, except to the extent the information can be provided in the necessary course of business of the Company, acting reasonably including to provide HOC Entities with information to assist the HOC Entities and their advisors with the preparation of the financial statements for such HOC Entities, nothing herein shall require the Company to provide HOC Entities with any information which would constitute a material fact with respect to the Company which has not been generally disclosed.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company will endeavor to permit representatives of the HOC Entities to have some access to the site and some of the premises where the business and operations of the Company and its Subsidiaries are conducted, and limited access to the Company and its Subsidiaries' books of account and records. The extent and nature of this access will be determined by the Company, considering its operational needs.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing 'shall' with 'will endeavor to permit some access' and adding 'The extent and nature of this access will be determined by the Company, considering its operational needs,' the obligation is significantly weakened. This gives the company wide discretion to limit access, undermining the original intent of providing meaningful access to HOC. This can be used to selectively deny access, undermining compliance with the Sarbanes-Oxley Act.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 404 of Sarbanes-Oxley requires companies to establish and maintain internal controls over financial reporting. Adequate access to information is crucial for assessing these controls. By ambiguously limiting HOC's access, the company may hinder HOC's ability to evaluate internal controls, potentially masking weaknesses and contradicting the spirit and intent of SOX 404, particularly if HOC has board representation and oversight responsibilities.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 98 / SUBCHAPTER IV / § 7262 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC 7262 : Management assessment of internal controls Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 15-COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 98-PUBLIC COMPANY ACCOUNTING REFORM AND CORPORATE RESPONSIBILITY SUBCHAPTER IV-ENHANCED FINANCIAL DISCLOSURES Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §7262. Management assessment of internal controls (a) Rules required The Commission shall prescribe rules requiring each annual report required by section 78m(a) or 78o(d) of this title to contain an internal control report, which shall- (1) state the responsibility of management for establishing and maintaining an adequate internal control stru
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting for the company and management's assessment of the effectiveness of such controls. The rules do, however, afford a company the flexibility to design its internal control over financial reporting to fit its particular circumstances. We believe that it would be inconsistent with the purposes of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act to specify different requirements for small entities. VIII. STATUTORY AUTHORITY AND TEXT OF RULE AMENDMENTS The amendments described in this release are being adopted under the authority set forth in Sections 5, 6, 7, 10, 17 and 19 of the Securities Act, as amended, Sections 12, 13, 15, 23 and 36 of the Exchange Act, Sections 8, 30, 31 and 38 of the Investment Company Act, as amended and Sections 3(a), 302, 404, 405 and 906 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act. List of Subjects 17 CFR Part 210 Accountants, Accounting, Reporting and recordkeeping requirements, Securities. 17 CFR Part 228 Reporting and recordkeeping requirements, Securities, Small businesses. 17 CFR Parts 229, 240 and 249 Reporting and recordkeeping requirements, Securities. 17 CFR Parts 270 and 274 Investment companies, Reporting and recordkeeping requirements, Securities. TEXT OF AMENDMENTS For the reasons set out in the preamble, the Commission amends title 17, chapter II, of the Code of Federal Regulations as follows: PART 210 - FORM AND CONTENT OF AND REQUIREMENTS FOR FINANCIAL STATEMENTS, SECURITIES ACT OF 1933, SECURITIES EXCHANGE ACT OF 1934, PUBLIC UTILITY HOLDING COMPANY ACT OF 1935, INVESTMENT COMPANY ACT OF 1940, INVESTMENT ADVISERS ACT OF 1940, AND ENERGY POLICY AND CONSERVATION ACT OF 1975 1. The authority citation for Part 210 is revised to read as follows: Authority : 15 U.S.C. 77f, 77g, 77h, 77j, 77s, 77z-2, 77z-3, 77aa(25), 77aa(26), 78c, 78j-1, 78 l , 78m, 78n, 78o(d), 78q, 78u-5, 78w(a), 78 ll , 78mm, 79e(b), 79j(a), 79n, 79t(a), 80a-8, 80a-20, 80a-29, 80a-30, 80a-31, 80a-37(a), 80b-3, 80b-11, 7202 and 7262, unless otherwise noted. 2. Section 210.1-02 is amended by: a. Removing the authority citation following §210.1-02; b. Redesignating paragraph (a) as paragraph (a)(1); and c. Adding paragraph (a)(2). The revisions read as follows: §210.1-02 Definition of terms used in Regulation S-X (17 CFR part 210). * * * * * (a)(1) * * * (2) Attestation report on management's assessment of internal control over financial reporting . The term attestation report on management's assessment of internal control over financial reporting means a report in which a registered public accounting firm expresses an opinion, or states that an opinion cannot be expressed, concerning management's assessment of the effectiveness of the registrant's internal control over financial reporting (as defined in §240.13a-15(f) or 240.15d-15(f) of this chapter) in accordance with standards on attestation engagements. When an overall opinion cannot be expressed, the registered public accounting firm must state why it is unable to express such an opinion. * * * * * 3. Amend §210.2-02 by: a. Revising the section heading; b. Revising the headings of paragraphs (a), (b), (c) and (d); and c. Adding paragraph (f). The addition and revisions read as follows. §210.2-02 Accountants' reports and attestation reports on management's assessment of internal control over financial reporting. (a) Technical requirements for accountants' reports . * * * (b) Representations as to the audit included in accountants' reports . * * * (c) Opinions to be expressed in accountants' reports . * * * (d) Exceptions identified in accountants' reports . * * * * * * * * (f) Attestation report on management's assessment of internal control over financial reporting . Every registered public accounting firm that issues or prepares an accountant's report for a registrant, other than an investment company registered under section 8 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 (15 U.S.C. 80a-8), that is included in an annual report required by sectio
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates access to the company's site, premises, books, and records for HOC Entities holding at least 14.5% of shares, while the changed text weakens this obligation by stating that the Company 'will endeavor to permit some access' and that 'The extent and nature of this access will be determined by the Company, considering its operational needs.' This shift from a mandatory 'shall' to a discretionary 'will endeavor' significantly undermines the initial obligation. The law snippets address the importance of internal controls. Snippet 1 references 15 USC 7262 regarding management's responsibility for internal controls. Snippet 2 also discusses 'establishing and maintaining adequate internal control over financial reporting.' While neither snippet explicitly states 'access', the provided Law Explanation argues that limited access impedes HOC's ability to evaluate internal controls, which is crucial under SOX 404. The changed text creates ambiguity and company discretion, potentially hindering HOC's oversight and contradicting the intent of SOX to ensure adequate internal controls over financial reporting. This ambiguity makes the change a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Company will indemnify and save harmless the HOC Entities and the directors, officers, employees and agents of the HOC Entities (collectively, the "HOC Indemnitees") from and against all Claims incurred by any one or more of the HOC Indemnitees directly or indirectly resulting from any breach of any covenant, representation or warranty of the Company contained in this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Company may, at its discretion, indemnify and save harmless the HOC Entities and their personnel from Claims resulting from breaches of covenants, representations, or warranties, provided the Company deems such indemnification reasonable and necessary.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'will indemnify' with 'may, at its discretion, indemnify' makes indemnification optional rather than mandatory. The added condition that the Company must deem indemnification 'reasonable and necessary' introduces a subjective element, allowing the Company to avoid indemnification even in cases where a clear breach has occurred. This weakens the protection afforded to HOC.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Colorado law allows for indemnification, C.R.S. § 7-109-505 outlines specific circumstances where indemnification is permissible for directors and officers, including acting in good faith and with a reasonable belief that their conduct was in the corporation's best interests. The modified clause gives the company broad, discretionary power to deny indemnification even if the HOC Indemnitees meet the statutory requirements for indemnification, essentially allowing the company to override the protections intended by Colorado law. The original text provides a more straight forward requirement for indemnification, where the change makes it discretionary, weakening HOC's protections under the agreement.
perturbation.justification: The modification of 'will indemnify' to 'may, at its discretion, indemnify' introduces an ambiguity that undermines the original obligation. The original text provides a clear requirement for indemnification, while the change makes it discretionary, weakening the protections. The law explanation suggests this change could allow the company to circumvent indemnification requirements that might otherwise apply, especially if the HOC indemnitees meet statutory requirements for indemnification, which falls under the Ambiguity Legal Contradiction definition.
